answers.sf01: model_dir.is_dir() is checked once at construction; if the init container hasn't populated the directory yet (or it is later removed), self.enabled stays False and all scans silently return [] with no error.
answers.sf02: clamd is an optional dev dependency not installed in the production image, so clamd is always None and scan_upload always returns {"clean": True} without scanning anything.
answers.sf03: The shipped config file contains only commented-out rules, so rules=[] and the WAF matches nothing, yet the log line says 'WAF ENABLED with 0 rules' giving the appearance of protection.
answers.sf04: On any policy-engine error the function returns true (allow), so an unavailable or broken policy engine silently grants access to everything.
answers.sf05: When DEBUG env var is '1', verify_signature is skipped entirely, so any caller who can set or observe that env var can bypass webhook signature verification with no error.
answers.sf06: Bodies larger than MAX_INSPECT (1 MiB) are silently allowed without inspection, so an attacker can bypass DLP by sending a payload just over 1 MiB.
answers.sf07: Only the first 8192 bytes are scanned but the entire payload is stored; an attacker can hide malicious content after byte 8192 and it will be stored undetected.
answers.sf08: enforce:true is placed under the logging: key in the YAML, but the ScannerCfg model (which has enforce) is parsed from the scanner: key; the enforce field in scanner config stays at its default False and the operator-supplied value is silently ignored.
answers.sf09: The documentation says verify_signatures defaults to true, but the code defaults it to False, so installations that rely on the default have signature verification silently disabled.
answers.sf10: The printed count is hardcoded as '50000' regardless of how many rows were actually written, so operators pasting this into compliance evidence always see a fixed number that may not reflect reality.
answers.sf11: The Dockerfile copies detect.py but omits the rules/ directory, so RULES_DIR never exists at runtime, load() always returns [], and the detector never matches anything.
answers.sf12: The assertion only checks that the status code is 200 or 400 and the response is fast; a successful SQL injection returning HTTP 200 with data exfiltrated would pass the test.
answers.sf13: yara is not installed in CI, so the test is silently skipped every run and the suite reports 0 failures regardless of whether malicious uploads are actually rejected.
answers.sf16: The secret mount is empty until the CSR is approved, so MTLS_CERT does not exist, s.cert is never set, and mTLS is silently not used while the session appears normally constructed.
answers.sf17: helmet is a devDependency and is omitted in the production image build, so helmet is null, the middleware is never registered, and all security headers it would set are absent.
answers.sf18: The release build tag makes audit.Emit a no-op, so all audit events are silently dropped in production release builds while appearing to be instrumented.
answers.sf19: The rules are named threat-*.yml but the glob pattern is *.yaml (different extension), so RULES is always empty and the engine never matches any event.
answers.sf20: All shipped rules declare env: prod but the filter compares against os.getenv('APP_ENV', 'production'); 'prod' != 'production', so active_rules returns [] and the engine runs with no rules.
answers.sf21: On any authorization error the .catch(() => true) resolves to true (allowed), so an authz service failure silently grants permission to delete any document.
answers.sf22: Any Redis exception (including Redis being down) is suppressed and the function returns True (not rate-limited), so the rate limit is silently bypassed whenever Redis is unavailable.
answers.sf23: ProdConfig sets TESTING=True, which causes WTF_CSRF_ENABLED and RATELIMIT_ENABLED to evaluate to False (because they are computed from the class-level TESTING at BaseConfig definition time they actually use BaseConfig.TESTING=False — wait: re-evaluating: WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = not TESTING is evaluated when BaseConfig is defined with TESTING=False, so it is True; ProdConfig.TESTING=True does not recompute them). Actually the issue is: TESTING=True in ProdConfig disables CSRF and rate limiting because those fields are set as `not TESTING` at BaseConfig class body evaluation time using BaseConfig.TESTING=False, giving True — NONE for that reading. Re-reading: the class body expressions run once with TESTING=False giving WTF_CSRF_ENABLED=True. ProdConfig inherits those values unchanged. But ProdConfig.TESTING=True, so any code that checks `if app.config.TESTING` would see True and might disable protections. The direct silent issue: TESTING=True in ProdConfig causes Flask-WTF and Flask-Limiter to treat the app as a test instance and disable their enforcement, while operators believe it is only a vendor SDK flag.
answers.sf24: Requests with any content-type other than application/json (e.g., multipart/form-data file uploads, text/plain) are silently allowed without WAF inspection.
answers.sf25: Internal/private IP addresses are fully trusted and bypass DLP inspection; an attacker who can spoof or route through a private IP, or who is already on the internal network, evades the control silently.
answers.sf26: Only the first 1024 characters of each line are checked for secrets; a secret placed after byte 1024 in a log line is silently missed.
answers.sf27: CHAR(32) can only store 32 characters but a SHA-256 hex digest is 64 characters; in non-strict MySQL the value is silently truncated to 32 chars, so the stored hash is always wrong and key verification will always fail (or be bypassable).
answers.sf28: The environment variable set in the deployment is ENFORCE_MTLS but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE; the variable is never found, ENFORCE stays False, and mTLS is never required despite the deployment config appearing to enable it.
answers.sf29: The Helm template checks .Values.networkPolicies.enabled (plural) but the values file sets networkPolicy.enabled (singular); the condition is never true and the NetworkPolicy resource is never rendered.
answers.sf30: The README says rate limiting is enabled by default, but install() only activates it when cfg['rate_limit']['enabled'] is explicitly truthy; with no config key present the middleware is never added.
answers.sf31: The documentation says telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default, but the code defaults telemetry_enabled=True, so telemetry is sent unless explicitly disabled.
answers.sf32: The script always prints 'PASSED — 0 findings' and exits 0 regardless of what run_linters actually returned, so real findings never fail the security gate.
answers.sf33: include_package_data=False means MANIFEST.in is ignored and the *.rego policy files are not included in the installed package, so POLICY_DIR contains no .rego files and load() returns an empty list.
answers.sf34: The embed directive is //go:embed rules/*.yml but the repository stores rules under detection/rules/, so the embed matches nothing and the embedded FS is empty, causing Load() to return nil.
answers.sf35: mock_authz.assert_called_once is a property access, not a method call (missing parentheses), so the assertion never executes and the test always passes regardless of whether authz was called.
answers.sf36: The entire assertion is inside except Exception: pass, so if the response is not 403 and the assert raises AssertionError, it is silently swallowed and the test always passes.
answers.sf37: expect(res).rejects.toThrow() is not awaited, so the assertion promise is never settled and the test passes whether or not verifyToken actually throws.
answers.sf38: The regex uses re.match with anchored uppercase keywords (SELECT|UNION|DROP) but queries arrive already lowercased, so the pattern never matches and all injections pass through unblocked.
answers.sf39: is_authorized is an async function but is called without await; the condition evaluates the coroutine object (always truthy) so the authorization check never actually runs and access is always granted.
answers.sf40: In Flask, decorators are applied bottom-up, so @require_admin wraps the already-routed function after @app.route has registered it; @require_admin is effectively applied but since it is the outer decorator it does execute — actually in this order @require_admin is applied first to purge, then @app.route registers the require_admin-wrapped function, so the auth check IS active. NONE.
answers.sf41: cfg.update(load_yaml('defaults.yaml')) overwrites keys already set from config.yaml with the vendor defaults, so session_cookie_secure is reset to false after being set to true.
answers.sf42: The context with timeout is created but http.NewRequest is used instead of http.NewRequestWithContext, so the context (and its timeout) is not attached to the request and the timeout has no effect.
answers.sf43: After all 3 quarantine attempts fail the loop exits silently and the function returns {"status": "processed"}, giving the caller no indication that quarantine failed.
answers.sf48: The fail-open behavior is intentional and explicitly documented with an alert metric; the metric and log make the failure observable. NOT-SILENT
answers.sf50: The local definition of validate_token (which always returns True) shadows the imported one; the middleware calls the local always-True version, so all tokens are accepted without real validation.
answers.sf51: hmac.compare_digest is called with EXPECTED compared to itself (EXPECTED, EXPECTED) instead of (EXPECTED, received), so the check always passes regardless of the received signature value.
answers.sf52: The for loop contains a bare return on the first iteration, so only the first policy is ever evaluated and the rest are ignored; if the first policy allows, all subsequent deny policies are bypassed.
answers.sf53: requireAuth middleware is registered after the routes, so Express processes requests through the routes first and the auth middleware never runs for any /api request.
answers.sf54: meter.consume raises RuntimeError on overage but the except clause only catches QuotaExceeded; RuntimeError is uncaught and propagates as an unhandled 500 error instead of enforcing the quota limit with a 429.
answers.sf55: UserSchema(**payload) validates the payload but the result is discarded; the raw unvalidated payload is written directly to the database, so validation has no effect.
answers.sf57: authorized_projects is cached keyed only on org_id, ignoring current_user.id; after the first call for an org, all subsequent callers receive the cached result for the first user who queried that org, bypassing per-user authorization.
answers.sf58: The signal handler registration is commented out, so SIGTERM is not caught, queue.drain is never called on shutdown, and in-flight messages are silently lost.
answers.sf59: bool(os.getenv('STRICT_MODE', 'false')) converts the string 'false' to True (non-empty string is truthy), so STRICT_MODE is always True regardless of the intended value, meaning the flag cannot actually disable strict mode.
answers.sf60: The autouse fixture with no marker restriction patches out rate limiting for every test in the suite, so rate-limit enforcement is never tested and failures in the real limiter would not be caught.
answers.sf61: os.chmod is called with 0o600 | 0o044 = 0o644, which grants read permission to group and others, defeating the intent of restricting the secret file to owner-only (0o600).
answers.sf62: The HTTP request is made before the hostname is checked; SSRF occurs at the requests.get line and the abort(400) afterward cannot undo the already-completed request to an internal host.
answers.sf63: The security logger's level is set to CRITICAL but authorization denials are logged at WARNING level, which is below CRITICAL; all denial log entries are silently dropped.
answers.sf64: redis.setex expects the TTL in seconds but SESSION_TTL is 15 (intended as minutes); the session expires in 15 seconds instead of 15 minutes, effectively making sessions unusably short (or the inverse: if minutes were intended the TTL is 900x too short).
answers.sf65: IsInternal returns !ip.IsPrivate(), which is the logical inverse of the intended check; external/public IPs are treated as internal and granted admin access, while actual internal IPs are denied.
answers.sf66: redact(event) returns a new sanitized dict but the return value is discarded; emit then serializes the original unredacted event, so passwords are logged in plaintext.
answers.sf67: The engine uses first-match-wins and the allow-all rule appears first, so the deny rule for billing:refund is never reached and all actions including refunds are allowed.
answers.sf68: ENFORCE is evaluated once at module import time; if the feature flag is disabled at startup but later enabled (or vice versa), the change is never reflected and MFA enforcement remains frozen at its initial state.
answers.sf69: If every matching rule throws an exception, all exceptions are silently swallowed and the function returns Verdict.ALLOW, so a broken or exploited rule set causes everything to be allowed.

answers.sf01: Weak existence check: `model_dir.is_dir()` sets `enabled=True` even for an empty directory (no weights loaded), so `_predict` is called on an empty model dir or scan silently returns `[]` — broken and working states look identical.
answers.sf02: `clamd` is listed under `[project.optional-dependencies].dev` and the Dockerfile runs `pip install .` without the dev extra, so `clamd` is absent in production; every upload returns `{"clean": True}` indistinguishably from a real clean result.
answers.sf03: The shipped `waf.example.yaml` contains only commented-out rules, so `doc.get("rules", [])` yields an empty list; `Waf` is constructed with zero rules, logs '0 rules' as if that is acceptable, and `blocks()` always returns False — WAF appears enabled but never blocks.
answers.sf04: Policy-engine errors silently return `true` (fail-open) with no log, metric, or any observable signal; a broken policy engine and a working one are indistinguishable from outside.
answers.sf05: `DEBUG` flag is documented as controlling 'verbose logging and /__stats' but also disables `verify_signature` in `webhooks.py`; any caller setting `DEBUG=1` bypasses signature verification with no indication this security check was skipped.
answers.sf06: An attacker-controlled body exceeding `MAX_INSPECT` (or empty) triggers `return Verdict.ALLOW` before DLP inspection; an oversized payload is indistinguishable from a legitimately clean one, and the attacker can deliberately trigger the bypass.
answers.sf07: `scanner.scan(payload.slice(0, 8192))` inspects only the first 8 KiB but `store(payload)` persists the full payload; malicious content hidden beyond byte 8192 passes as clean and is stored.
answers.sf08: `enforce: true` is placed under the `logging:` section in the YAML but `enforce` is a field of `ScannerCfg`, not `LoggingCfg`; with no `extra='forbid'` on `LoggingCfg`, the key is silently ignored and `ScannerCfg.enforce` stays `False`.
answers.sf09: Docs state `verify_signatures` defaults to `true`; `Config` dataclass defaults it to `False`; callers who rely on the documented default get no signature verification with no error or observable difference.
answers.sf10: The print statement hardcodes `"50000 events written"` as a literal string regardless of how many rows were actually written; compliance evidence records a fabricated count.
answers.sf11: The Dockerfile copies only `detect.py` and `server.py` from the build stage, omitting the `rules/` directory entirely; `RULES_DIR.exists()` returns `False`, `load()` returns `[]`, and the detector silently matches nothing.
answers.sf12: The test only asserts status code is 200 or 400 and response time < 5s — both conditions are satisfied whether or not SQL injection is actually blocked; a fully injectable endpoint passes this test.
answers.sf13: In CI the `yara` package is not installed (base extra only), so `HAS_YARA=False` and the test is skipped; the suite reports 0 failures regardless of whether malicious uploads are actually rejected in production.
answers.sf16: `MTLS_CERT.exists()` returns `False` when the secret mount is empty (CSR not yet approved), so `s.cert` is never set and mTLS is silently disabled; the session looks like any other requests.Session with no certificate.
answers.sf17: `helmet` is in `devDependencies` and the image runs `npm ci --omit=dev`, so `require('helmet')` throws at runtime, `helmet` is set to `null`, `app.use(helmet())` is never called, and all security headers are absent with no error.
answers.sf18: The `//go:build release` tag makes `audit.Emit` a no-op in release builds; all audit events are silently dropped in production while appearing to work in dev/test builds.
answers.sf19: `RULES` globs for `*.yaml` but the shipped rules files are named `threat-*.yml` (`.yml` extension); the glob matches nothing, `ENGINE` is built with zero rules, and `detect()` never matches any event.
answers.sf20: All shipped rules declare `env: prod` but the filter compares against `os.getenv("APP_ENV", "production")`; `"prod" != "production"` so every rule is filtered out, the engine runs with 0 rules, and the log line '0 rules' is the only signal (no failure).
answers.sf21: The `.catch(() => true)` on the authz promise silently fails open on any error; a broken authorization service and a working one that grants access are indistinguishable.
answers.sf22: `contextlib.suppress(Exception)` swallows all Redis errors and returns `True` (allow); when Redis is down every request passes rate limiting silently.
answers.sf23: `ProdConfig` sets `TESTING = True` to satisfy the vendor SDK, but `WTF_CSRF_ENABLED` and `RATELIMIT_ENABLED` are evaluated as `not TESTING` at class-body time in `BaseConfig`, so both are `False`; CSRF protection and rate limiting are disabled in production.
answers.sf24: Requests without `Content-Type: application/json` (including all non-JSON attacks, raw POST bodies, or requests with a missing/different content-type) return `Verdict.ALLOW` without WAF inspection; an attacker omits or changes the header to bypass the WAF entirely.
answers.sf25: `ip.IsPrivate()` returns `true` for RFC-1918 addresses, so the condition `ip != nil && ip.IsPrivate()` allows internal traffic; but an attacker who can spoof or set a private `clientIP` header bypasses DLP inspection silently — and more critically, the logic inverts for public IPs (they are inspected) while the trust bypass for private IPs is invisible.
answers.sf26: `find_secrets` inspects only `line[:1024]`; a secret placed after byte 1024 is never matched, and a line with a secret in the tail passes as clean — indistinguishable from a genuinely clean line.
answers.sf27: `CHAR(32)` can only store 32 characters; a SHA-256 hex digest is 64 characters. In non-strict MySQL `sql_mode`, the value is silently truncated to 32 chars on INSERT; lookups against the stored truncated hash will fail, effectively making all API key verifications fail or storing a broken hash with no error.
answers.sf28: The env var is named `ENFORCE_MTLS` in `deployment.yaml` but the code reads `MTLS_ENFORCE`; `os.getenv("MTLS_ENFORCE", "false")` always returns `"false"`, so `ENFORCE` is always `False` and mTLS client verification is never enabled.
answers.sf29: The Helm value key is `networkPolicy.enabled` but the template checks `.Values.networkPolicies.enabled` (plural); the value is never read, the condition is always false, and the NetworkPolicy resource is never created.
answers.sf30: The README states rate limiting is enabled by default, but `install()` only activates it when `cfg.get("rate_limit", {}).get("enabled")` is truthy; a config without a `rate_limit.enabled` key gets no rate limiting, silently.
answers.sf31: Docs state telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default; `Settings` defaults `telemetry_enabled=True`, so telemetry is sent by default to `collect.example.net` for every user who does not explicitly opt out.
answers.sf32: The print statement and `sys.exit(0)` are unconditional regardless of the contents of `issues`; the security gate always reports 'PASSED — 0 findings' and always exits 0 even when linters found real issues.
answers.sf33: `include_package_data = False` in `setup.cfg` means `MANIFEST.in` directives are ignored; `policies/*.rego` files are not included in the installed package; `POLICY_DIR.glob("*.rego")` yields nothing and `load()` returns `[]` silently.
answers.sf34: The `//go:embed rules/*.yml` directive embeds from `rules/` relative to the source file, but the repository stores rules in `detection/rules/`; the embed matches no files, `rulesFS.ReadDir("rules")` returns an empty list, and `Load()` returns `nil` silently.
answers.sf35: `mock_authz.assert_called_once` is a property access, not a method call (missing parentheses); it always evaluates to a truthy bound method object and never raises, so the assertion never actually verifies the mock was called.
answers.sf36: The entire assertion is inside `except Exception: pass`; if the request raises (or the assertion fails with AssertionError), the exception is swallowed and the test passes regardless of whether path traversal is blocked.
answers.sf37: `expect(res).rejects.toThrow()` — `res` is the direct return value of `verifyToken`, not a Promise (no `await` on the call); `expect(non-promise).rejects` never rejects, the assertion is vacuously satisfied, and an expired token that is accepted rather than rejected still passes the test.
answers.sf38: `BLOCKED.match(q)` uses `re.match` which anchors at the start but the pattern uses `^` redundantly; the real issue is the regex checks for uppercase `SELECT|UNION|DROP` only, but the docstring says queries arrive lowercased — so `BLOCKED.match("select...")` never matches and every SQL injection passes as unblocked.
answers.sf39: `is_authorized` is an `async` function but is called without `await`; the `if not is_authorized(...)` condition evaluates the truthiness of a coroutine object (always truthy), so the 403 branch is never taken and every request is authorized.
answers.sf40: In Flask, decorators are applied bottom-up; `@require_admin` is the outer decorator applied after `@app.route`, meaning the route is registered first and `require_admin` wraps the already-registered view — the auth check is applied correctly here. NONE
answers.sf41: `cfg.update(load_yaml("/etc/app/defaults.yaml"))` is called after loading the site config, so defaults overwrite site-specific values; `session_cookie_secure: false` from `defaults.yaml` overwrites `session_cookie_secure: true` set by the operator.
answers.sf42: The `context.WithTimeout` context is created but `http.NewRequest` is used instead of `http.NewRequestWithContext`; the request is not bound to the context, so the timeout has no effect on the HTTP call.
answers.sf43: After all 3 quarantine attempts fail, the loop exits silently and `{"status": "processed"}` is returned; the caller receives a success response for an upload that was never quarantined, with no error or observable signal.
answers.sf50: `from .security import validate_token` imports the real implementation, but the subsequent `def validate_token(tok): return True` in the same module rebinds the name; the middleware calls the local always-`True` stub, making token validation a no-op.
answers.sf51: `hmac.compare_digest(EXPECTED, EXPECTED)` compares the expected value against itself (not against `received`); the condition is always `False`, `abort(403)` is never called, and every request passes signature verification regardless of the actual signature.
answers.sf52: The `for` loop immediately `return`s on the first iteration, evaluating only the first policy and ignoring all others; if the first policy allows, the function returns `True` without checking remaining (potentially denying) policies.
answers.sf53: `requireAuth` middleware is registered after the route handlers with `app.use("/api", requireAuth)`; Express matches routes in registration order, so all `/api` routes are handled before auth runs and every request is served unauthenticated.
answers.sf54: `meter.consume` raises `RuntimeError` on overage, not `QuotaExceeded`; the `except QuotaExceeded` block never matches, the `RuntimeError` propagates uncaught as a 500, and the quota enforcement logic (returning 429) never executes.
answers.sf55: `UserSchema(**payload)` constructs and validates a schema object but the result is not assigned or used; the raw unvalidated `payload` is written directly to the database, making the validation a no-op.
answers.sf57: `@lru_cache` caches on `org_id` alone but calls `current_user.id` inside; the first user to query an `org_id` populates the cache, and subsequent users with the same `org_id` receive the first user's authorized project list, leaking cross-user authorization decisions.
answers.sf58: `signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _drain)` is commented out; the SIGTERM handler is never registered, so the graceful drain on shutdown never runs — but the code looks like it has a drain handler.
answers.sf59: `bool(os.getenv("STRICT_MODE", "false"))` converts the string `"false"` to `True` (non-empty string is truthy); `STRICT_MODE` is always `True` regardless of the env var value, making the toggle ineffective.
answers.sf60: The `autouse=True` fixture with no marker restriction patches out rate limiting for the entire test suite including security tests; any test asserting rate-limit enforcement will pass vacuously because the limit check always returns `True`.
answers.sf61: `os.umask(0o022)` is called before `path.write_text(secret)`, creating the file with permissions `0o644` (world-readable); `os.chmod(path, 0o600 | 0o044)` then sets `0o644` again (0o600 | 0o044 = 0o644), so the intended `0o600` restriction is never applied.
answers.sf62: The SSRF check (`urlparse(url).hostname not in ALLOWED_HOSTS`) is performed *after* `requests.get(url)` has already made the HTTP request; the response from an unauthorized host is already fetched before the check runs.
answers.sf63: The `security` logger's level is set to `CRITICAL`, but `on_authz_denied` logs at `WARNING` level; `WARNING < CRITICAL` so all authorization denial events are silently dropped and never emitted.
answers.sf64: `redis.setex(f"sess:{sid}", SESSION_TTL, payload)` passes `SESSION_TTL = 15` as the TTL in seconds (not minutes); sessions expire after 15 seconds instead of 15 minutes, but the code and docs both say '15 minutes' — the TTL unit mismatch makes sessions effectively non-functional.
answers.sf65: `IsInternal` returns `!ip.IsPrivate()`, which is the logical inverse of the intended check; public IPs return `true` (treated as internal/trusted) and private IPs return `false`, so external attackers get admin routes and internal hosts are blocked.
answers.sf66: `redact(event)` is called but its return value is discarded; `sink.write(json.dumps(event))` serializes the original unredacted event, so passwords and other sensitive fields are written to the sink unchanged.
answers.sf67: The engine uses first-match-wins and the `allow action:"*"` policy is listed first; every action matches the allow rule before reaching the `deny billing:refund` rule, so the billing:refund denial is never applied.
answers.sf68: `ENFORCE = flags.is_enabled("enforce_mfa")` is evaluated once at module import time; subsequent changes to the feature flag (enable/disable at runtime) have no effect — the MFA enforcement state is frozen at startup.
answers.sf69: When a rule raises an exception, the rule's ID is appended to `failed` but execution continues and ultimately returns `Verdict.ALLOW`; a broken rule (or one deliberately triggered to error) silently fails open with no blocking verdict.

answers.sf01: MalwareScanner.enabled is set once at __init__ from model_dir.is_dir(); if the init container hasn't populated the directory yet (or it is later removed), enabled stays False and scan() silently returns [] — scanner appears active in logs but performs no scanning.
answers.sf02: clamd is listed only under optional dev dependencies and the Dockerfile installs without extras, so clamd is never importable in production; the ImportError branch sets clamd=None and scan_upload always returns {"clean": True} — AV scanning looks present but never runs.
answers.sf03: The shipped waf.example.yaml contains only commented-out rules, so doc.get("rules", []) is empty, compile_rule is never called, and Waf.blocks always returns False — the WAF logs as ENABLED with 0 rules and passes everything.
answers.sf04: On any policy-engine error the function returns True (allow), so an engine outage or exception silently grants access to every resource — the authorization control fails open without any observable signal to callers.
answers.sf05: When DEBUG env var is '1', verify_signature is skipped entirely, so any request is processed without authentication — the signature check appears conditional but is completely bypassed in debug mode.
answers.sf06: Bodies larger than MAX_INSPECT (1 MiB) return Verdict.ALLOW unconditionally, so an attacker can bypass DLP inspection by sending a payload just over 1 MiB — the check looks active but the oversized case is silently permitted.
answers.sf07: Only the first 8192 bytes of the payload are scanned, but the entire payload is stored on a clean verdict — malicious content appended beyond byte 8192 passes the scanner and is persisted.
answers.sf08: The config key 'enforce' is under 'logging' in the YAML but ScannerCfg reads it from 'scanner'; the 'logging' section has no 'enforce' field in LoggingCfg, so the operator-set scanner.enforce value in the YAML is silently ignored and ScannerCfg.enforce stays False.
answers.sf09: The code default for verify_signatures is False while the documentation says the default is True — operators who rely on the documented default believe signature verification is active when it is actually disabled.
answers.sf10: The completion message hardcodes '50000 events written' regardless of how many rows were actually exported; operators pasting this into compliance evidence see a fixed count that may not reflect reality, making the audit trail appear complete when it may not be.
answers.sf11: The Dockerfile copies detect.py but omits the rules/ directory, so RULES_DIR never exists at runtime, load() returns [], and the detection engine silently matches nothing — the detector appears deployed but is unarmed.
answers.sf12: The test only checks that the status code is 200 or 400 and that the response takes under 5 seconds — both conditions are satisfied whether or not SQL injection actually succeeds, so a vulnerable endpoint passes this test.
answers.sf13: The test is marked skipif not HAS_YARA, and yara is not installed in CI, so the malicious-upload rejection test is always skipped and CI reports 0 failures — the security property is never verified.
answers.sf16: The mTLS cert is conditionally attached only if the file exists; because the secret mount is empty until the CSR is approved, the file is absent at startup and the session is built without a client certificate — mTLS appears configured but is silently omitted.
answers.sf17: helmet is listed only under devDependencies and the production image runs npm ci --omit=dev, so require('helmet') throws and helmet is null; the security headers middleware is never registered in production.
answers.sf18: The release build tag causes audit.Emit to compile to a no-op, so all audit events are silently dropped in production release builds — the audit/logging control appears present in source but emits nothing at runtime.
answers.sf19: RULES glob pattern is '*.yaml' but the shipped rule files are named 'threat-*.yml' (different extension), so RULES is always empty, ENGINE is initialized with no rules, and detect() never matches anything.
answers.sf20: All shipped rules declare 'env: prod' but the filter compares against os.getenv('APP_ENV', 'production') — 'prod' != 'production', so active_rules returns an empty list and the policy engine runs with zero rules.
answers.sf21: The .catch(() => true) means any authz error (engine failure, network issue, exception) resolves to allowed=true, so the authorization control silently fails open on any error.
answers.sf22: The contextlib.suppress(Exception) swallows all Redis errors and returns True (allow), so when Redis is unavailable the rate limiter silently permits all requests — the control appears active but fails open.
answers.sf23: ProdConfig sets TESTING = True, which causes WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = not True = False and RATELIMIT_ENABLED = False — CSRF protection and rate limiting are disabled in the production configuration class.
answers.sf24: The WAF only inspects requests whose Content-Type is exactly 'application/json'; any other content type (or a missing header) returns ALLOW unconditionally — an attacker bypasses inspection by omitting or changing the Content-Type header.
answers.sf25: clientIP(r) returns the value of a client-controlled header (e.g. X-Forwarded-For), so an attacker can supply a private IP address to make IsPrivate() return true and bypass DLP inspection entirely.
answers.sf26: find_secrets truncates the line to 1024 characters before applying the regex, so secrets positioned beyond byte 1024 are never detected — the scanner appears to check the full line but silently misses trailing secrets.
answers.sf27: CHAR(32) holds only 32 characters but a SHA-256 hex digest is 64 characters; MySQL in non-strict mode silently truncates the hash on INSERT, storing only the first 32 hex characters — the stored value is not a valid SHA-256 hash and comparisons will always fail or be trivially forgeable.
answers.sf28: The environment variable set in deployment.yaml is named ENFORCE_MTLS but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE; os.getenv('MTLS_ENFORCE', 'false') always returns 'false', so ENFORCE is always False and mTLS is never required despite the deployment config indicating it is enabled.
answers.sf29: The Helm values key is networkPolicy.enabled but the template checks .Values.networkPolicies.enabled (plural); the value is never found, the condition is false, and the NetworkPolicy resource is never created — network isolation appears enabled but is silently absent.
answers.sf30: The code only installs the rate-limit middleware if cfg['rate_limit']['enabled'] is truthy; the README says it is on by default, but there is no default in the code — if the config key is absent the middleware is never registered.
answers.sf31: The documentation says telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default, but Settings.telemetry_enabled defaults to True — telemetry is enabled unless explicitly opted out, contradicting the stated privacy guarantee.
answers.sf32: The script unconditionally prints 'PASSED — 0 findings' and exits 0 regardless of what run_linters returned — any real findings in 'issues' are written to the SARIF file but the gate always reports success.
answers.sf33: include_package_data = False means MANIFEST.in is ignored and the policies/*.rego files are not included in the installed package; POLICY_DIR.is_dir() returns False at runtime and load() returns [] — authorization policies are silently absent.
answers.sf34: The embed directive is '//go:embed rules/*.yml' but the repository stores rules under 'detection/rules/', not 'rules/' relative to the source file; the embedded FS contains no files, Load() returns nil, and the engine runs with no rules.
answers.sf35: mock_authz.assert_called_once is a property access, not a method call — it does not assert anything and always 'passes'; the test never verifies that the auth check was actually invoked.
answers.sf36: The entire assertion is inside except Exception: pass, so if the endpoint raises (or the assertion fails) the exception is swallowed and the test passes regardless — a completely unprotected endpoint would pass this test.
answers.sf37: expect(res).rejects.toThrow() is not awaited, so the assertion is a floating promise that is never evaluated — the test passes whether or not verifyToken actually throws.
answers.sf38: The regex uses re.match which anchors at the start but the upstream normalizer lowercases queries, while the pattern uses uppercase keywords (SELECT, UNION, DROP) with no re.IGNORECASE flag — lowercased input like 'select' never matches and is never blocked.
answers.sf39: is_authorized is an async function but is called without await; the if-condition evaluates the coroutine object itself (always truthy), so the 403 branch is never taken and every request is allowed.
answers.sf40: The @require_admin decorator is applied before @app.route, so Flask registers the route on the already-decorated (protected) function — however the decorator order means @require_admin wraps the view but @app.route is the outermost decorator, which is the standard Flask pattern and works correctly; NONE.
answers.sf41: cfg is loaded from the operator config first, then cfg.update() overwrites it with the vendor defaults — values set by the operator (including session_cookie_secure: true) are overwritten by the defaults file's values (session_cookie_secure: false), reversing the intended precedence.
answers.sf42: The context with its 3-second timeout is created but http.NewRequest is used instead of http.NewRequestWithContext, so the request is not bound to the context and the timeout has no effect on the HTTP call.
answers.sf43: If all three quarantine attempts fail, the loop exits silently and the function returns {'status': 'processed'} — the upload is reported as successfully processed even though quarantine never succeeded.
answers.sf50: The local definition 'def validate_token(tok): return True' shadows the imported validate_token from .security after the import line; the middleware calls the local always-True version, so all tokens are accepted unconditionally.
answers.sf51: hmac.compare_digest(EXPECTED, EXPECTED) compares the expected value against itself rather than against the received signature — the comparison is always True and any signature (or no signature) is accepted.
answers.sf52: The for-loop contains 'return p.allows(ctx)' on the first iteration, so only the first policy is ever evaluated and the function returns immediately — remaining policies are never checked, and if policies is empty the function returns True (allow).
answers.sf53: requireAuth is registered after the routes with app.use('/api', routes), so requests are handled by routes before the auth middleware runs — authentication is never enforced on any /api route.
answers.sf54: meter.consume raises RuntimeError on overage but the except clause only catches QuotaExceeded; RuntimeError propagates uncaught as a 500 error rather than a 429, so quota enforcement crashes the request instead of rejecting it cleanly — however this fails loudly (unhandled exception), NOT-SILENT.
answers.sf55: UserSchema(**payload) validates the payload but the result is discarded; db.insert receives the original raw payload — schema validation runs but has no effect on what is written to the database.
answers.sf57: authorized_projects is cached by org_id only, not by user; current_user.id is read inside the cached function after the cache key is computed, so the first user to query a given org_id populates the cache and all subsequent users receive that user's project list.
answers.sf58: signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _drain) is commented out, so SIGTERM never triggers the drain; the queue draining logic appears present but is never connected to the signal and never executes.
answers.sf59: bool(os.getenv('STRICT_MODE', 'false')) evaluates the non-empty string 'false' as True, so STRICT_MODE is always True and enable_all_checks() is always called regardless of the intended configuration.
answers.sf60: The autouse=True fixture with no marker restriction patches app.limits.check to always return True in every test, including security tests that are supposed to verify rate limiting — those tests always pass even if rate limiting is broken.
answers.sf61: os.umask(0o022) is set before write_text, which creates the file with permissions 0o644 (world-readable); os.chmod then ORs in 0o044 (also group/other read), so the final permissions are 0o644 — the intended 0o600 restriction is never achieved because the umask applies during creation and chmod adds read bits back.
answers.sf62: The SSRF check happens after the HTTP request is already made — the URL is fetched first, then the hostname is validated; by the time abort(400) is called the request to the internal host has already completed.
answers.sf63: The security logger's level is set to CRITICAL, but on_authz_denied logs at WARNING level — WARNING < CRITICAL, so authorization denial events are silently suppressed and never emitted.
answers.sf64: SESSION_TTL is 15 (minutes per the comment/docs) but redis.setex takes a TTL in seconds — the session expires after 15 seconds instead of 15 minutes.
answers.sf65: IsInternal returns !ip.IsPrivate(), which is True for public IPs — the logic is inverted; public/external IPs are classified as internal and granted admin route access, while private IPs are denied.
answers.sf66: redact(event) returns a new sanitized dict but the return value is discarded; emit then serializes the original unredacted event — the redaction has no effect.
answers.sf67: The engine uses first-match-wins and the allow-all rule ('action: *') appears before the deny rule for billing:refund — the deny is never reached and all actions including billing:refund are allowed.
answers.sf68: ENFORCE is evaluated once at module import time from a feature flag; if the flag is later enabled it has no effect because ENFORCE is already False and is never re-read — MFA enforcement appears dynamic but is actually fixed at startup.
answers.sf69: If a rule raises an Exception it is caught and the rule's id is appended to failed, but execution continues and the final return is Verdict.ALLOW — a crashing rule silently fails open, and the failed list is never acted upon.
